phases.name: 00_scaffold
phases.content:
# Phase 0 — Scaffold

## Objective
Bootstrap the SvelteKit + Vite project with all tooling configured and the GitHub connectivity layer working. By the end of this phase, the app loads, accepts a GitHub PAT, and can list the contents of `/images/` in the configured repo. No upload, annotation, or GIF features yet.

## Tasks

### SvelteKit + Vite project setup
- [ ] Initialize a SvelteKit project with Vite, using `@sveltejs/adapter-static` (static site generation)
- [ ] Configure DaisyUI + Tailwind CSS (DaisyUI for UI components/styling, Tailwind under the hood)
- [ ] Set up Tailwind config with DaisyUI plugin
- [ ] Remove any server-side features (no SSR, no server endpoints; fully static output)
- [ ] Add `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` for GitHub Pages deployment

### GitHub connectivity
- [ ] Build

flow:
    - [ ] Show a screen explaining the required scope (repo write access) in plain language
    - [ ] Provide a deep-link to `github.com/settings/tokens/new?scopes=repo&description=Emmy`
    - [ ] Include a text field to paste the generated token back in
    - [ ] On submit, store the PAT in `localStorage` (persists across browser sessions indefinitely)
- [ ] Document the localStorage tradeoff plainly in the UI: persisting a write-scoped token in localStorage is more exposed to XSS than session-only; accepted because this is a single-user tool
- [ ] Gallery browsing requires no auth at all — gate only write actions behind the connect flow

### Octokit client wrapper
- [ ] Create `src/lib/github.js` — module wrapping Octokit with functions:
    - [ ] `getFile(owner, repo, path)` — fetch a single file from the repo
    - [ ] `putFile(owner, repo, path, content, message)` — create or update a file
    - [ ] `listDirectory(owner, repo, path)` — list contents of a directory
    - [ ] `searchCode(owner, repo, query)` — search repo/code (used in later phases)

### Verify end-to-end
- [ ] App loads and displays the connect screen (or gallery if already connected)
- [ ] Entering a valid PAT allows listing the contents of `/images/` in the configured repo

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] App loads in browser without errors
- [x] Token stored in localStorage survives a full tab close/reopen (no re-auth on return)
- [x] GitHub Actions deploy workflow is present and runs successfully on push
- [x] Gallery page renders with zero auth required when no token is saved
- [x] With a valid PAT, the app can call the GitHub API and list files under `/images/`
- [x] Invalid or missing PAT shows a clear error message to the user

phases.name: 01_upload_and_gallery
phases.content:
# Phase 1 — Upload + Gallery Read

## Objective
Users can upload images (with client-side resize/compress), browse a gallery of all stored images, filter by tag, and copy a working direct link. The gallery fetches image metadata efficiently — either via a generated index or direct API fetches.

## Tasks

### Upload flow
- [ ] Implement file picker (drag-and-drop + click-to-browse)
- [ ] Client-side resize/compress before upload:
    - [ ] Cap longest edge at 2000px max
    - [ ] Compress to reasonable file size to avoid unbounded repo bloat
- [ ] Generate a unique ID for each upload (pattern: `YYYY-MM-DD-<random-suffix>`, e.g. `2026-07-11-a1b2c3`)
- [ ] Upload image file to `/images/<id>.<ext>` via Octokit `putFile`
- [ ] Upload matching JSON metadata file to `/images/<id>.json` (flat, per-data-model shape)
- [ ] Commit both files together (or in rapid succession — same batch commit if API supports it)

### Gallery view
- [ ] Decide between two strategies and document choice in a code comment:
    - **(a) GitHub Action**: Action rebuilds a generated `index.json` on push; gallery reads that one file (fewer API calls, better rate-limit behavior)
    - **(b) Client-side fetch**: fetch directory listing + all JSON files directly via API (zero CI complexity)
- [ ] Fetch and render all entries under `/images/`
- [ ] Display each image as a card/thumbnail with filename, tags, caption, and direct link
- [ ] Show direct link (`raw.githubusercontent.com/...`) per image with a copy button

### Tag filter and search
- [ ] Implement client-side tag filtering (no server calls)
- [ ] Implement basic text search over filenames, tags, and captions
- [ ] Filter/search is instant — operates over already-loaded metadata

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] User can upload an image via drag-and-drop or file picker
- [x] Uploaded image appears in the gallery immediately (or after a refresh)
- [x] User can filter gallery by tag
- [x] User can search images by filename/tag/caption text
- [x] User can copy a working direct link to any image (resolves via raw.githubusercontent.com)
- [x] Each image has a JSON metadata file alongside it in the repo
- [x] Gallery works without a PAT (read-only mode)
- [x] Upload (a write action) requires a valid PAT — shows connect screen if missing

phases.name: 02_annotation_layer
phases.content:
# Phase 2 — Annotation Layer

## Objective
Add a per-image annotation editor that lays arrows, boxes, and text over images as an SVG overlay — stored non-destructively in the JSON metadata `annotations` field. Boxes and text must support transparent fill (outline-only / no background). Annotations persist across reloads and render live over images in both the editor and the gallery detail view.

## Tasks

### Data model
- [ ] Extend the JSON metadata schema to include an `annotations` field (SVG elements serialized as JSON array, or null)
- [ ] Each annotation shape has: type ("arrow", "box", "text", optionally "freehand"), position/size, color, stroke width, fill (with alpha support for transparent)
- [ ] Store annotations in the image's already-existing JSON file under `annotations` — reuse the same Octokit `putFile` wrapper

### Annotation editor UI
- [ ] Build an image detail/editor view (separate page or modal from gallery)
- [ ] Render the base image on a canvas or SVG container
- [ ] Layer a transparent SVG overlay on top for drawing/editing
- [ ] Tool palette: arrow tool, box tool, text tool
- [ ] Boxes and text must support transparent fill:
    - [ ] Toggle between filled (opaque background) and outline-only (transparent fill)
    - [ ] Alpha-aware rendering — not just an opaque rect with a border
- [ ] Optional (if feasible): freehand drawing tool
- [ ] Each tool allows picking color and stroke width
- [ ] Draggable/resizable shapes after placement
- [ ] Delete shape action

### Save and reload
- [ ]

button serializes current SVG shapes into the JSON `annotations` field
- [ ] Commits updated JSON back to the repo via Octokit
- [ ]

navigates back to gallery
- [ ] Re-opening the editor for the same image loads and re-renders the saved annotations identically

### Gallery detail view integration
- [ ] In the gallery, clicking an image opens a detail view showing the full image
- [ ] Annotations render on top of the image in the detail view (read-only)
- [ ] An

button opens the annotation editor

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] User can open an image and see it with an empty overlay
- [x] User can draw arrows, boxes, and text on the image
- [x] Boxes and text support transparent fill (outline-only — no opaque background blocking the image)
- [x] User can toggle between filled and transparent fill on boxes/text
- [x] Annotations survive a page reload (saved to repo, loaded back)
- [x] Gallery detail view shows annotations burned onto the image
- [x] Re-opening the editor restores the same editable shapes

phases.name: 03_slideshow_and_gif_export
phases.content:
# Phase 3 — Slideshow Builder + GIF Export

## Objective
Users can arrange a sequence of annotated images into a timed slideshow and export it as a single animated GIF. The GIF rasterizes each slide (image + annotations burned in with alpha support), encodes via `gifenc`, and commits the result back into `/images/` as a taggable gallery entry with source traceability.

## Tasks

### Slideshow builder
- [ ] Build a slideshow composer UI:
    - [ ] Pick an ordered sequence of images from the gallery (target: ~6–7 images typical — don't over-engineer for hundreds)
    - [ ] Reorder slides via drag-and-drop or up/down buttons
    - [ ] Set per-slide duration (default ~2–3 seconds, editable per slide)
    - [ ] Remove a slide from the sequence
- [ ] In-app slideshow player:
    - [ ] Play through slides with the configured timing
    - [ ] Pause button to freeze on a slide
    - [ ] While paused, allow inspecting/editing that slide's annotations (link through to Phase 2 editor)

### GIF export pipeline
- [ ] Create `src/lib/gif-export.js` — dedicated wrapper module for GIF encoding
- [ ] Per-frame rasterization:
    - [ ] For each slide, draw the image onto a `<canvas>`
    - [ ] Draw the annotation SVG overlay on top, rasterized with alpha support (respect transparent fill — boxes/text with no background must not occlude the image behind)
    - [ ] Annotations are burned into the frame (GIF has no overlay/layer concept)
- [ ] Encode frames using **gifenc** (better palette quantization for sharp UI/text edges than alternatives):
    - [ ] Add `gifenc` as a dependency
    - [ ] Keep encoder behind the small wrapper module (`src/lib/gif-export.js`) so it's swappable later
- [ ] Size guard:
    - [ ] Before encoding, calculate total resolution × frame count
    - [ ] If result looks like it'll produce an oversized file, downscale frames and/or warn the user
- [ ] On export completion:
    - [ ] Commit the resulting `.gif` to `/images/` as a new entry (same ID pattern or new ID)
    - [ ] Write matching JSON metadata with `type: "gif"` and `sourceSlideIds` set to the ordered list of source image IDs
- [ ] Offer a plain download button for the generated GIF (before committing, in case user wants it locally without pushing to the repo)

### Gallery integration
- [ ] GIF entries appear in the gallery alongside images
- [ ] GIF entries are taggable, filterable, and have direct links just like images
- [ ] Detail view for a GIF shows its metadata including `sourceSlideIds` (traceability back to source images)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] User can select 6–7 images and arrange them in order
- [x] User can set per-slide durations
- [x] In-app player plays through slides and can be paused
- [x] While paused, user can inspect/edit a slide's annotations
- [x] Export produces a valid animated GIF
- [x] Annotations are burned into each frame with transparent fill respected (outline-only boxes/text don't block the image)
- [x] GIF is committed to the repo as a gallery entry with `type: "gif"`
- [x] GIF entry's metadata contains `sourceSlideIds` linking back to source images
- [x] GIF appears in the gallery, is taggable, filterable, and has a direct link
- [x] User can also download the GIF locally without committing

context:
## Where this came from (narrative / philosophy)

This idea arrived in the half-asleep stretch between sleep and waking, and the first move was reaching for a phone and hitting the microphone button — not the keyboard — to talk it through with an AI before coffee, before anything had a chance to knock it loose.

That distinction is the actual discipline worth keeping, more than anything technical in this doc: typing is composition, and composition is a lossy compression format — the moment you start finding words to type, you're already condensing and editing instead of getting the raw thought out. Talking skips that step. Even so, parts of it evaporated in real time while trying to get it out — a half-formed idea behaves like fog, not like a file. The habit to build isn't "have good ideas while waking up." It's: the instant something half-formed shows up, reach for voice before anything that forces composition, and talk it out before it's gone.

---

## What this is (design intent / product philosophy)

Emmy is a personal, lightweight image-hosting and sharing tool. It replaces Imgur for one person's workflow: upload a screenshot fast, tag it, get a shareable link, and — its actual differentiating feature — stitch several screenshots into an annotated GIF for posting to Twitter/X, which caps native image posts at 4 but allows a single GIF of any sequence length.

It runs entirely on GitHub as infrastructure: a public GitHub repo is the database, GitHub Pages is the host, and the GitHub REST API (via Octokit) is the only backend that exists. There is no server, no database, no user accounts beyond the owner's own GitHub account.

**This is explicitly a single-user, personal tool.** Not a SaaS, not multi-tenant. If someone else wants it, they fork the repo and run their own instance with their own token. Do not build any shared/multi-user abstractions — that's out of scope and would be actively wrong to add.

**There is no public/private toggle and never will be.** Everything uploaded through this tool is intentionally public — the user already has Google Photos for private storage. Do not build a visibility setting, a private mode, or gate viewing behind auth. The GitHub PAT is only ever needed for *write* actions (upload, tag edit, annotate, export). Browsing/viewing the gallery requires no auth at all.

---

## Non-negotiable technical constraints

- **JavaScript + JSDoc only. No TypeScript, ever.** Use JSDoc comments for IDE type hinting. Do not add a `tsconfig.json`, do not use `.ts`/`.tsx` files, do not suggest migrating to TypeScript at any point, even partially.
- Stack: **SvelteKit + Vite**, static adapter (`@sveltejs/adapter-static`), deployed to GitHub Pages. **DaisyUI** for UI components/styling on top of Tailwind.
- **Octokit.js** for all GitHub API calls (upload, commit, fetch, search).
- No backend server, no database beyond the git repo itself, no ORM.
- Fully client-side. Every feature must work from a static site with a user-supplied GitHub PAT — no serverless functions in v1.
- **Unix philosophy: do one thing well.** This tool hosts, tags, links, and turns screenshots into GIFs. It deliberately does not do AI-powered search, smart albums, or content understanding — the owner's Google Photos already covers that for the private/full library. Emmy is the public-share layer, not a Photos competitor. Do not add AI captioning/tagging/vision features; this is a considered scope boundary, not a gap to fill later.

### Auth persistence
- On first connect, the PAT is stored in **localStorage**, not sessionStorage — it persists across browser sessions indefinitely ("one and done"), not re-auth every time the tab closes. This is a single-user tool; the owner logs in once.
- Provide a "Connect GitHub" flow: explain the required scope (repo write access) in plain language, deep-link to GitHub's token creation page with the scope pre-selected via URL params if possible (e.g. `github.com/settings/tokens/new?scopes=repo&description=Emmy`), then a field to paste the generated token back in.
- Document this tradeoff plainly in the UI or README: persisting a write-scoped token in localStorage is more exposed to XSS than a session-only token would be. Accepted here because this is a single-user tool with a small, owner-controlled codebase — not a reason to skip mentioning it.

---

## Data model

One image = one committed image file + one JSON metadata file, same base name, committed together.

```
/images/
  2026-07-11-a1b2c3.png
  2026-07-11-a1b2c3.json
  2026-07-11-a1b2c3.gif        <- GIF exports live here too, same pattern
  2026-07-11-a1b2c3.gif.json
```

Metadata JSON shape (adjust as needed, but keep it flat and simple):

```json
{
  "id": "2026-07-11-a1b2c3",
  "filename": "2026-07-11-a1b2c3.png",
  "type": "image",
  "createdAt": "2026-07-11T14:22:00Z",
  "tags": ["screenshot", "app-flow"],
  "caption": "",
  "width": 1200,
  "height": 800,
  "annotations": null,
  "sourceSlideIds": null
}
```

- `type` is `"image"` or `"gif"`.
- `annotations` holds the SVG overlay JSON for that image, or `null`.
- `sourceSlideIds` (GIF entries only) lists the image ids that were combined to produce this GIF, in order — this is what gives you rollback/traceability on GIF exports for free, same idea as the metadata-JSON-per-image pattern.

**Why JSON-per-image instead of one big index file:** git history on each file gives free rollback per image without touching unrelated entries. Do not collapse this into a single monolithic `gallery.json` — that defeats the whole point of the design.

---

## Explicitly out of scope for this build (v2 / deferred — do not implement)

Do not build these. They are documented here only so you don't accidentally start down these paths while reasoning about "what would a complete version need."

- **AI-powered tagging, captioning, or content search.** Rejected by design, not deferred — see the Unix-philosophy note in §2. Google Photos already does this for the owner's full library; Emmy is deliberately just the public-share layer.
- **Comments with visitor identity.** Would require GitHub OAuth, which needs a client-secret exchange that can't happen from static JS — i.e. a serverless function, real infra this project deliberately avoids. If this is ever wanted, the answer is embedding **giscus** (GitHub Discussions-backed, OAuth already solved elsewhere), not a custom-built Worker + Issues mapping.
- **Per-image OG preview pages** (so raw links unfurl into image cards on Twitter/Slack/Discord via `og:image` meta tags). Not needed for the GIF-attach workflow, which uses native media upload, not a link at all.
- Any multi-user, shared-account, or permissions system.
- A private/public visibility toggle of any kind.

---

## Operational caveats — surface these to the user, don't just silently note them

- **GitHub Pages requires a public repo** on free/non-Enterprise plans — everything uploaded is public, which matches the design intent, but the agent building this should not add any UI implying otherwise.
- **Bandwidth/abuse risk is about traffic volume, not which feature exists.** GitHub's Acceptable Use Policy allows throttling, suspension, or repo deletion if usage is judged excessive relative to other users (typically with advance notice). This is a personal/moderate-traffic tool, not a CDN. If a comment or docstring needs to reference this, keep it factual and brief — don't build any enforcement logic around it, it's just user awareness.
- **Recommend the user run this under a separate GitHub account or organization** from their primary one, so any future flag/throttle on this repo doesn't touch their main identity or other repos. This can be a line in the README, not code.

---

## Notes for the implementing agent

- Keep each phase's diff reviewable. Don't refactor earlier phases while implementing later ones unless something is actually broken.
- Favor small, composable modules (`src/lib/github.js`, `src/lib/gif-export.js`, `src/lib/annotations.js`, etc.) over one large file — this will be picked up and continued by other agent runs across sessions, so legibility matters more than cleverness.
- If you hit a design decision not covered here, make the simplest choice consistent with the constraints, note the choice in a code comment, and move on — don't block on it.
